critique.summary: The article introduces the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP), an open standard co-developed by Stripe and Tempo to enable autonomous agents to make payments programmatically. It explains how MPP facilitates seamless transactions for agents using Stripe's infrastructure, supporting various payment methods including stablecoins and fiat currencies.
mainPoints: AI agents are evolving to perform complex tasks autonomously and require the ability to transact with businesses and other agents.
Current financial systems are designed for humans, making it difficult for agents to complete purchases without human intervention.
MPP is an open standard that allows agents to coordinate payments programmatically, supporting microtransactions and recurring payments.
Stripe users can integrate MPP easily using the PaymentIntents API and accept payments in stablecoins, fiat, and buy now, pay later methods.
MPP is already in use by businesses such as Browserbase, PostalForm, Prospect Butcher Co., and Stripe Climate to enable agent-driven transactions.
Payments made via MPP appear in Stripe's existing dashboard and benefit from Stripe's infrastructure including fraud protection and tax calculation.
Stripe is building a broader set of tools for the emerging agent economy, including the Agentic Commerce Suite and related protocols.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with publicly available information from Stripe's official communications and technology announcements. Stripe has publicly discussed the development of MPP and its role in enabling autonomous agent payments, and the examples provided align with known use cases. The technical details about integration with Stripe's PaymentIntents API and support for multiple payment methods are accurate based on Stripe's documented APIs. There is no evidence of misinformation or exaggeration in the article.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential security and privacy risks associated with autonomous agent payments.
It does not address regulatory or compliance challenges that may arise from machine-to-machine financial transactions.
The scalability and adoption rate of MPP across different industries and regions is not covered.
Potential costs or fees for businesses using MPP are not mentioned.
The article does not explore how disputes or errors in agent-initiated payments would be handled.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP) and its implications for autonomous agents making payments. Commenters debate the novelty and utility of MPP compared to previous protocols, concerns about security and authorization, and potential use cases including automated research and decentralized finance.

comments.author: rvz
comments.text:
This is a good standard that I can get behind [0] since it's a serious proposal and submitted to the IETF [1] for MPP for machine-to-machine payments.

A well thought out proposal for the long term, unlike MCP which is a complete joke of a "standard" and broken by design.

[0] https://paymentauth.org/

[1] https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ryan-httpauth-payment...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-informed perspective, referencing the IETF submission and contrasting MPP with the earlier MCP, indicating a thoughtful and accurate assessment.

comments.author: btown
comments.text:
Jokes about wallet-draining aside, we're already giving our agents a real cash budget that they use for tokens. Our harnesses have mechanisms to manage that spend. And having an easily detectable protocol would allow the harness to ensure that its deterministic code is in play to make these payments - you'd give your payment details to the harness, not to the agent itself.

And as to use cases, if I want quality outputs for automated research and discovery of a topic, in a world where quality journalism/scholarship should be compensated and does use tools like Cloudflare to block automated access, and where AI-generated content is everywhere, it's optimal for me to want to spend some amount of the money I spend on tokens, on the ability for my agent to access reputable primary and secondary sources as needed.

The challenge, of course, is that now there's an incentive for a spam source to try to get my agent to pay it, rather than the actual creator of the content. But there are interesting ways to solve this, because with these payment rails there's now an incentive for alliances of content creators to maintain indices of reputable sources and their canonical domains - perhaps even authoritative hashes of content. Lots of possibilities here.
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully discusses practical mechanisms for managing agent payments and potential use cases, showing understanding of the technical and economic context.

comments.author: naomi_kynes
comments.text: MPP handles 'how do agents pay', but not 'did anyone authorize this'. For low-value API calls that's fine. But once agents start chaining transactions, you need a channel where the agent can ask a human 'I'm about to spend $2k on this, still in scope?' before the payment happens - not a fraud alert after. The authorization layer is a separate infrastructure problem from the payment protocol.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately highlights a critical limitation of MPP regarding authorization and fraud prevention, emphasizing the need for separate infrastructure.

comments.author: rickydroll
comments.text:
As soon as you have a fungible currency, I expect that, from past experience, everyone who carries those packets will stick out a hand for a piece of the action. From a brief read of the description, I also expect attackers to work out a way to drain your account without you noticing.

Regulation E limits your losses for electronic banking. Is this new payment system covered by Regulation E? What is the maximum loss a consumer would experience?
comments.analysis: Raises valid security and regulatory concerns about the new payment system, reflecting a cautious and realistic viewpoint.

comments.author: vicchenai
comments.text: the real question for me is what happens when agents start hitting premium data APIs with MPP. right now if i want my agent to pull realtime financial data it has to go through my API keys with monthly billing. with MPP the agent could theoretically pay per-query directly to data vendors. thats a much better model for bursty workloads but the authorization problem naomi_kynes raised is real - you need spending caps that the agent cant override, not just logging.
comments.analysis: Provides a practical concern about controlling agent spending on premium APIs, suggesting necessary safeguards.

comments.author: neya
comments.text:
I feel like the word "protocol", is just abused like it is a glorified marketing term. Kind of like how the word "hacker" was abused in everything else that had nothing to do with hacking.

MCP was just a glorified way of tool calling but generated so much hype (and it eventually died down). Now we have MPP. Which again - could have just been another tool call exposed to the agent.

Imagine you hire someone who claimed to have invented a new protocol and you're thinking of something like TCP or UDP, but all they share is just a markdown file.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about the term 'protocol' and the novelty of MPP, reflecting a critical but somewhat subjective viewpoint.

comments.author: vishnuharidas
comments.text: Okay, I am NEVER letting an agent make payment autonomously. If there's a payment that has to be made, tell me, I will do that myself.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a cautious and emotional reaction to autonomous payments, emphasizing user control.

comments.author: clawbridge
comments.text: Does this mean crypto won't be banking the agents like everyone's been saying?
comments.analysis: Raises a relevant question about the role of cryptocurrency in agent payments, indicating engagement with the topic.

comments.author: giovannibonetti
comments.text:
For those of you in Brazil, my company jota.ai has built a financial AI-assistant that you can chat with to open a bank account, connect with accounts from other banks, make instant Pix payments with any of them, all of that through WhatsApp right now. We're working hard to release long-running agents soon that can do increasingly complex workflows involving payments and whatnot.

Please let us know if you have suggestions of what complex workflows you would like to build.
comments.analysis: Shares a real-world example of AI-assisted payments, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: sutib
comments.text:
"they need the ability to transact with businesses and one another."

Really, they _need_ it. How can we possibly live without computers spending money without supervision?
comments.analysis: This comment uses sarcasm to question the necessity of autonomous payments, adding a humorous but less substantive perspective.
